attributes.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic one week after returning from a one-month backpacking trip through Southeast Asia, including Thailand, Vietnam, and Malaysia. She is concerned because she tried raw snails as a local delicacy and is worried she could have been exposed to an infectious agent. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She denies any unprotected sexual intercourse, intravenous drug use, tattoos or blood transfusions while overseas. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following pathogens is she most at risk of having acquired?
attributes.explanation:
Given this patient's history of consuming raw snails in Southeast Asia, she is at highest risk for infection with Angiostrongylus cantonensis, also known as rat lungworm. This parasitic nematode is endemic in Southeast Asia and can cause eosinophilic meningitis in humans who accidentally ingest infected intermediate hosts (raw snails or slugs) or parentenic hosts (prawns, frogs, lizards, fish or crabs) or contaminated produce (such as unwashed vegetables).

Hepatitis E virus is transmitted through the faecal-oral route and is common in developing countries. While it can be acquired through contaminated water or undercooked meat, raw snails are not a typical source of transmission.

Entamoeba histolytica causes amoebic dysentery and liver abscess. It is typically acquired through contaminated water and does not use snails as intermediate hosts.

Clonorchis sinensis, the Chinese liver fluke, is transmitted through consumption of raw or undercooked freshwater fish, not through snails.

Salmonella typhi, which causes typhoid fever, is transmitted through contaminated food and water but is not specifically associated with raw snail consumption.

The consumption of raw snails is particularly significant in this case as they are the primary intermediate hosts for A. cantonensis. When humans consume infected snails, the larvae can migrate to the central nervous system, potentially causing eosinophilic meningitis. Symptoms typically develop 1-3 weeks after exposure and include severe headache, neck stiffness, and parasthesias. Although neurological symptoms are most common, ocular infections and pulmonary signs can occur rarely.

Of note, although previously considered a travellers condition only, rat lungworm can also be found in areas of Eastern Australia, resulting in documented human cases in Queensland and New South Wales.

Other pathogens that patients may be at risk of when consuming uncooked snails include Paragonimus westermani (lung fluke), Fasciolopsis buski (giant intestinal fluke), Schistosomiasis and various bacterial infections such as Salmonella and Aeromonas spp. infection.
learningPoints: Angiostrongylus cantonensis (rat lungworm) can cause eosinophilic meningitis in humans who consume infected raw snails or contaminated produce (such as unwashed vegetables).
The rat lungworm lifecycle involves rats as definitive hosts and molluscs as intermediate hosts, with humans as accidental hosts.
Raw snail consumption is the primary transmission route for Angiostrongylus cantonensis in Southeast Asia.
references: Angiostrongyliasis, Neural. In: Centres for Disease Control and Prevention [online]. 2019. Available at: https://www.cdc.gov/dpdx/angiostrongyliasis_can/index.html

Ewers EC, Anisowicz SK. The potential danger of eating wild lettuce: a brief review of human rat lungworm infection. Hawaii J Med Public Health. 2014 Nov;73(11 Suppl 2):28-32. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4244900/
Rat lungworm disease factsheet. In: NSW Health [online]. 2019. Available at: https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/factsheets/Pages/rat-lung-worm.aspx
